name: Hadoop 2.x Architecture (YARN)
chatResponse: I will walk you through the Hadoop 2.x architecture, specifically focusing on how YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) decoupled resource management from data processing to enhance scalability and flexibility.

steps.title: The Storage Layer: HDFS
steps.content:
The **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** remains the storage backbone. In Hadoop 2.x, it consists of:
- **NameNode**: The master server that manages the file system namespace and regulates access to files. It stores metadata (file-to-block mapping).
- **DataNodes**: The worker nodes that store the actual data blocks and perform block creation, deletion, and replication upon instruction from the NameNode.
- **Secondary/Standby NameNode**: Introduced to provide High Availability (HA), preventing the NameNode from being a single point of failure.

steps.title: The Resource Layer: YARN
steps.content:
The biggest shift in 2.x was the introduction of **YARN**. It splits the functions of the old Hadoop 1.x JobTracker into two separate entities:
1. **Resource Manager (RM)**: The global authority that arbitrates resources among all competing applications.
2. **Application Master (AM)**: A per-application framework-specific library that negotiates resources from the RM and works with the Node Manager(s) to execute and monitor tasks.

steps.title: The Execution Flow
steps.content:
When a client submits an application:
- The **RM** allocates a container to start the **Application Master**.
- The **AM** manages the lifecycle of the job. It asks the RM for specific resource containers on worker nodes.
- On each worker node, the **Node Manager (NM)** manages the container's environment, monitoring resource usage (CPU, Memory) and reporting back to the RM.

steps.title: The Container Concept
steps.content: In Hadoop 2.x, a **Container** is a physical collection of resources (e.g., 2GB RAM, 1 CPU Core). Instead of fixed 'slots' (Map slots/Reduce slots) like in version 1.0, YARN uses these dynamic containers to run any type of processing task—be it MapReduce, Spark, or Giraph.

visualCode:
graph TD
  subgraph Client_Space [Client Layer]
    C[Client App] --> RM
  end

  subgraph Master_Nodes [Master Layer]
    RM[Resource Manager - Scheduler/ASM] 
    NN[NameNode - Metadata]
    SNN[Standby NameNode]
  end

  subgraph Worker_Node_1 [Worker Node A]
    NM1[Node Manager]
    AM[Application Master]
    DN1[DataNode]
    C1[Container - Task]
  end

  subgraph Worker_Node_2 [Worker Node B]
    NM2[Node Manager]
    DN2[DataNode]
    C2[Container - Task]
  end

  RM <--> NM1
  RM <--> NM2
  AM -- Request Resources --> RM
  AM -- Command Launch --> NM1
  AM -- Command Launch --> NM2
  NM1 --> C1
  NM2 --> C2
  NN -. Sync .- SNN
  NN --- DN1
  NN --- DN2

  style RM fill:#1e293b,stroke:#0ea5e9,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
  style NN fill:#1e293b,stroke:#f43f5e,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
